title: Fundamentals: Properties, Ideal Gas & Phase Change
summary: Start here. This lesson turns thermodynamics from a fog of symbols into a checklist: choose the system, name the properties, decide whether the substance behaves like an ideal gas or a real fluid, then use either $pV=mRT$ or the tables. The big win is knowing when $p$ and $T$ are enough, when they are not, and how quality $x$ locates a wet mixture inside the vapor dome.
objectives: Point at a problem and say what the system, boundary, surroundings, and universe are.
Separate closed, open, and isolated systems without mixing them up with adiabatic processes.
Recognize extensive, intensive, and specific properties, especially the lowercase per-kg properties $v,u,h,s$.
Use the state postulate: two independent intensive properties fix a simple compressible state.
Use $pV=mRT$ safely, with kelvin temperatures and the correct gas constant.
Read the vapor dome: subcooled liquid, saturated mixture, superheated vapor, and why quality $x$ exists.
Use saturation/superheated tables and linear interpolation without freezing up.

blocks.text: 1. The language: systems, boundaries, surroundings

blocks.title: Plain-English map
blocks.text: Think of fundamentals as learning the names of the pieces on the board before playing the game. Most mistakes here are not deep physics mistakes; they are choosing the wrong model. Ideal gas? Use $pV=mRT$. Water/R134a near boiling or condensation? Use tables and the vapor dome. Two-phase mixture? You need quality $x$, because $p$ and $T$ are glued together by saturation.

blocks.title: Zero-panic route for any state-property question

steps.label: Name the container
steps.text: Closed system means fixed mass. Open system means mass crosses the boundary. This choice decides which later equations are even allowed.

steps.label: Choose the substance model
steps.text: If the statement says ideal gas, air at ordinary conditions, or gives $R,c_p,c_v$, think ideal-gas law. If it gives water/R134a saturation data, think tables.

steps.label: Find the state
steps.text: For a simple compressible system, look for two independent intensive properties. Inside the vapor dome, $p$ and $T$ count as only one independent fact.

steps.label: If wet, use quality
steps.text: Inside the dome, interpolate by mass fraction: property $= (1-x)f + xg$. Quality is the vapor mass fraction, not a volume fraction.

steps.label: Watch units before touching numbers
steps.text: Gas-law temperature is kelvin. Table specific volume may be scaled. Pressure units must match $R$. This is where easy marks leak away.

blocks.text:
Thermodynamics is the science of **energy** — how it is stored, transferred, and converted. Before any equation, we must agree on *what we are studying*.

- **System**: whatever we choose to study (a quantity of matter, or a region of space).
- **Surroundings**: everything outside the system.
- **Boundary**: the real or imaginary surface separating the two. It may be **fixed** (rigid tank) or **movable** (piston).
- **Universe** = system + surroundings.

- **Closed system**: no *mass* crosses the boundary, so the mass $m$ is constant. Energy (heat, work) may still cross. Example: gas in a sealed piston–cylinder.
- **Open system (control volume)**: a mass flow rate $\dot m$ ($\mathrm{kg/s}$) *can* cross the boundary. Example: a turbine, a nozzle.
- **Isolated system**: a special closed system that exchanges *neither* mass nor energy with its surroundings.

blocks.title: Closed does NOT mean adiabatic
blocks.text: A common exam slip: students assume a closed system cannot exchange heat. **Closed** only forbids *mass* transfer. Heat and work are still allowed. A process with no heat transfer is called **adiabatic** — a different concept entirely.
2. Properties, state, and the state postulate

blocks.term: Property, state, extensive vs intensive vs specific
blocks.text:
A **property** is a macroscopic feature you can assign a number to at an instant, with no knowledge of past history (mass, volume, $T$, $p$, ...). The **state** is the condition of the system as described by its properties.

- **Extensive** property: proportional to the amount of matter; additive (mass $m$, volume $V$, energy $U$).
- **Intensive** property: independent of amount; not additive (pressure $p$, temperature $T$).
- **Specific** property: an extensive property divided by mass, e.g. specific volume $v=V/m$ ($\mathrm{m^3/kg}$). Specific properties are intensive.

blocks.title: Quick test: extensive or intensive?
blocks.text: Cut the system in half. Properties that **halve** ($m$, $V$, $U$, KE, PE) are extensive. Properties that **stay the same** ($p$, $T$, $v$, $u$, $h$) are intensive. Lowercase letters ($v$, $u$, $h$, $s$) denote *specific* (per-kg) quantities throughout this course.

blocks.title: State postulate
blocks.caption: For a **simple compressible system** (electrical, magnetic, gravitational, motion, surface-tension effects negligible), exactly two independent intensive properties fix the state — every other property is then determined.

blocks.text: "Independent" matters. Inside the vapor dome (two-phase mixture), $p$ and $T$ are **not** independent — they are linked by saturation. There you must pick, e.g., $(p, x)$ or $(T, v)$. The Gibbs phase rule $V=C-F+2$ confirms this: for water ($C=1$) with two phases ($F=2$), $V=1$, so a single intensive property is free.
3. The ideal-gas law
At low pressure (molecules far apart, no intermolecular forces), every gas obeys the same simple equation of state. This is the **ideal-gas model**.

blocks.title: Ideal-gas equation of state
blocks.tex: pV = mRT \qquad pv = RT \qquad p\bar v = \bar R\,T
blocks.caption: $R$ is the specific gas constant, $R=\bar R/MM$, with universal constant $\bar R = 8314\ \mathrm{J/(kmol\cdot K)}$. For air $R=287\ \mathrm{J/(kg\cdot K)}$. $\bar v$ is the molar specific volume. **$T$ must be in kelvin.**

blocks.title: Kelvin, always kelvin
blocks.text: $pV=mRT$ is only valid with **absolute temperature**: $T(\mathrm{K})=t(^{\circ}\mathrm{C})+273.15$. Plugging in $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is the single most common numerical mistake. (Temperature *differences*, e.g. in $c\,\Delta T$, are the same in K and $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, but the gas law needs absolute $T$.)

blocks.title: Worked: rigid tank heating
blocks.text:
An ideal gas at $p_1=2.5$ bar and $t_1=20\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is in a **rigid** tank and is heated until $p_2=5$ bar. Find $t_2$.

**Rigid + closed** $\Rightarrow$ $V$ and $m$ constant $\Rightarrow$ $v$ constant. Writing the gas law at both states and dividing:
$$\frac{p_2 V}{p_1 V}=\frac{mRT_2}{mRT_1}\;\Rightarrow\;\frac{T_2}{T_1}=\frac{p_2}{p_1}.$$
$T_1=20+273.15=293.15$ K, so $T_2=293.15\times\frac{5}{2.5}=586.3$ K $=313\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.

**Answer: $t_2 \approx 313\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$** (586 K). Note that doubling the pressure doubles the *absolute* $T$, not the Celsius value.
4. Internal energy & enthalpy of an ideal gas
In general $u=u(T,v)$ and $h=h(T,p)$. The beautiful result for an **ideal gas** (Joule's experiment) is that $u$ and $h$ depend on temperature *alone*:

blocks.title: Ideal-gas caloric relations
blocks.caption: Enthalpy is defined as $h=u+pv$ (or $H=U+pV$). For an ideal gas $pv=RT$, so $h=u(T)+RT$ is also a function of $T$ only.

blocks.title: $du=c_v dT$ holds for ANY ideal-gas process
blocks.text: Because $u=u(T)$ only, $\Delta u=c_v\Delta T$ is valid even in a constant-**pressure** process, and $\Delta h=c_p\Delta T$ is valid even in a constant-**volume** process. The subscript on $c$ records *how the heat was measured*, not which process you may use it in. (This is exactly quiz Q5.) Using $c_v$ for a $\Delta h$ or $c_p$ for a $\Delta u$ is a classic trap.

question.prompt: For an ideal gas, the relation $h_2-h_1=c_p(T_2-T_1)$ is valid:

options.content: for any process, because $h=h(T)$ for an ideal gas
only for a reversible adiabatic process

question.explanation: For an ideal gas enthalpy depends on temperature alone, $h=h(T)$, so $dh=c_p\,dT$ integrates to $\Delta h=c_p\Delta T$ no matter what the path is. **A** confuses the subscript $p$ with a process restriction — $c_p$ is just the property; the relation works for any path. **B** is wrong: if $T$ is constant then $\Delta h=0$, a special case, not a restriction. **D** invents a restriction that does not exist.
question.theory: Ideal-gas caloric equations: $u=u(T)$, $h=h(T)$, so $\Delta u=c_v\Delta T$ and $\Delta h=c_p\Delta T$ hold for every ideal-gas process.

blocks.text: 5. Real substances: the p-v-T surface and the vapor dome

Water is **not** an ideal gas — it condenses and boils. Its equilibrium states lie on a 3-D **$p$-$v$-$T$ surface**: $p=p(T,v)$. Projecting it onto the $p$-$v$ (or $T$-$v$) plane gives the famous **vapor dome**.

Three regions matter:
- **Subcooled (compressed) liquid** — left of the dome, $T<T_{sat}$.
- **Two-phase mixture (wet vapor)** — *inside* the dome, liquid + vapor coexist.
- **Superheated vapor** — right of the dome, $T>T_{sat}$.

- **Saturation state**: the state at which a phase change starts or ends (the dome's borders).
- **Saturated-liquid line** (left border, subscript $f$) and **saturated-vapor line** (right border, subscript $g$).
- **Saturation temperature/pressure**: $T_{sat}$ and $p_{sat}$ are linked one-to-one. At a given $p$, boiling happens at a fixed $T_{sat}$.
- **Critical point** ($T_c=373.95\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, $p_c\approx220$ bar for water): top of the dome, where $f$ and $g$ lines meet. Above it, no distinct liquid/vapor.

blocks.title: The key two-phase fact
blocks.text: **Inside the dome, a constant-pressure process is also a constant-temperature process.** While liquid boils into vapor at $p$ = const, $T$ stays pinned at $T_{sat}(p)$ and only $v$ increases. That is why $p$ and $T$ are *not* independent there — you need quality $x$ to say *where* you are between $f$ and $g$.

blocks.title: p–v diagram explorer
blocks.caption: Trace a constant-pressure heating line through the vapor dome: watch $T$ stay flat at $T_{sat}$ while $v$ marches from $v_f$ to $v_g$, then climb again in the superheated region. Use it to *see* why $(p,T)$ cannot fix a two-phase state.

blocks.text: 6. Quality x and mixture properties

For a two-phase mixture, **quality** is the vapor mass fraction:
$$x=\frac{m_{vap}}{m_{liq}+m_{vap}},\qquad 0\le x\le1.$$
$x=0$ $\Rightarrow$ saturated liquid (all $f$); $x=1$ $\Rightarrow$ saturated vapor (all $g$). $x=0.75$ means 75% of the *mass* is vapor.

blocks.title: Any mixture property by mass-averaging
blocks.caption: Same recipe for $v,u,h,s$. Here $h_{fg}=h_g-h_f$ is the latent heat (enthalpy of vaporization). Read $f$ and $g$ values from the **saturation table** at the given $p$ or $T$.

blocks.title: How to locate a state and read the tables

steps.label: Step 1
steps.text: Identify the substance and which two properties you are given (e.g. $p$ and $v$, or $T$ and $x$).

steps.label: Step 2
steps.text: Go to the **saturation table** at the given $p$ (or $T$) and read $v_f$ and $v_g$. **Watch units**: in the Polito tables specific volumes are tabulated $\times10^3$, so $v_f$ for water near 1 bar is about $1.0\times10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^3/kg}$, $v_g\approx1.69\ \mathrm{m^3/kg}$.

steps.label: Step 3
steps.text: Compare your given $v$ to $v_f$ and $v_g$. If $v<v_f$: subcooled liquid. If $v_f\le v\le v_g$: two-phase — compute $x=\dfrac{v-v_f}{v_g-v_f}$. If $v>v_g$: superheated — use the superheated-vapor table instead.

steps.label: Step 4
steps.text: For two-phase, get every other property from Eq. 1.5. For single-phase off the table grid, **interpolate** linearly between the two bracketing rows.

blocks.title: Linear interpolation
blocks.caption: To find property $y$ at value $x$ lying between table rows $(x_1,y_1)$ and $(x_2,y_2)$. Works for reading $h$ at an in-between $T$, or $T_{sat}$ at an in-between $p$, etc.

blocks.title: Worked: heat to fully vaporize wet steam
blocks.text:
A tank holds 1 kg of saturated liquid–vapor water at $p=1$ bar, quality $x_A=0.75$. From the saturation table $h_f=417$ kJ/kg, $h_g=2675$ kJ/kg. How much heat at **constant pressure** turns it all into saturated vapor?

At constant pressure the heat equals the enthalpy change: $Q=m(h_g-h_A)$.
$$h_A=h_f+x_A(h_g-h_f)=417+0.75(2258)=2110.5\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}.$$
$$Q=m\,(h_g-h_A)=1\times(2675-2110.5)=564.5\ \mathrm{kJ}.$$
Shortcut: $Q=m(1-x_A)h_{fg}=1\times0.25\times2258=564.5$ kJ. **Answer: 564.5 kJ** (this is quiz Q8).

question.prompt: Water at 10 bar has $v_f=1.13\times10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^3/kg}$ and $v_g=0.1944\ \mathrm{m^3/kg}$. A rigid vessel holds it at $v=0.0978\ \mathrm{m^3/kg}$, 10 bar. The quality is closest to:
question.explanation: $x=\dfrac{v-v_f}{v_g-v_f}=\dfrac{0.0978-0.00113}{0.1944-0.00113}=\dfrac{0.09667}{0.19327}\approx0.50$. **B** comes from forgetting to subtract $v_f$ in the numerator and denominator scaling. **C** is wrong because $v<v_g$, so it is inside the dome, not superheated. **D** inverts the fraction (using $v_f$-ish over $v_g$). Since $v$ sits almost exactly halfway between $v_f$ and $v_g$, $x\approx0.5$.
question.theory: For a two-phase state, $v=(1-x)v_f+x v_g$ $\Rightarrow$ $x=(v-v_f)/(v_g-v_f)$. Always check $v_f\le v\le v_g$ first to confirm you are inside the dome.

blocks.text: 7. Putting it together: which model do I use?

blocks.title: Decision rule
blocks.text:
- **Gas far from its dome** (air, $\mathrm{N_2}$, combustion products, high $T$ / low $p$): use the **ideal-gas** model, $pV=mRT$, $\Delta u=c_v\Delta T$, $\Delta h=c_p\Delta T$.
- **Water or refrigerant near phase change** (steam cycles, fridges): use **TABLES**. Fix the state with two independent properties, find the region, interpolate or apply quality.
Never apply $pV=mRT$ to wet steam, and never apply quality to a superheated gas.

That is the whole foundation. From here, the First Law ($Q_{12}-W_{12}=U_2-U_1$) and entropy ride on top of these two ways of getting properties. If you can confidently (a) convert to kelvin and use $pV=mRT$, and (b) locate a state in the steam tables and find $x$, you are ready for everything that follows.

practice.prompt: A system is defined as **closed**. Which statement is necessarily true?

options.content: No heat can cross its boundary (it is adiabatic).
Its mass is constant; energy may still cross the boundary.
Neither mass nor energy can cross the boundary.
It must be at steady state.

practice.explanation: A closed system forbids only **mass** transfer, so $m$ is constant, while heat and work (energy) may still cross. **A** describes adiabatic, a separate concept. **C** describes an isolated system. **D** is unrelated — a closed system can be transient (e.g. a heating tank).
practice.theory: Closed = no mass transfer. Isolated = no mass AND no energy. Adiabatic = no heat (but work allowed).

practice.explanation: Pressure, temperature, and specific volume ($v=V/m$) are all independent of system size, hence intensive. **A** lists three extensive quantities. **C** mixes the extensive mass and KE with intensive pressure. **D** includes extensive $H$ and $V$. Test: split the system — intensive values are unchanged.
practice.theory: Extensive properties scale with mass ($m$, $V$, $U$, $H$, KE, PE); intensive do not ($p$, $T$). Specific (per-kg) properties are intensive.

practice.prompt: The **state postulate** for a simple compressible substance says the state is fixed by two independent intensive properties. Inside the vapor dome, which pair fails to fix the state?
practice.explanation: Inside the dome $p$ and $T$ are linked by saturation ($T=T_{sat}(p)$), so they are **not independent** and cannot together locate the state. **A**, **B**, **D** each pair a saturation variable with an independent one ($x$ or $v$) that tells you *where* between $f$ and $g$ you are, so they work.
practice.theory: Two-phase region: $p$ and $T$ are dependent. Use $(p,x)$, $(T,x)$, $(p,v)$, or $(T,v)$ to fix the state. Gibbs rule $V=C-F+2=1$ for water with 2 phases.

practice.prompt: A mass of ideal gas at 2.5 bar and 20 $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is in a **rigid** tank and heated until $p=5$ bar. The final temperature is:
practice.explanation: Rigid closed $\Rightarrow$ $v$ const $\Rightarrow$ $T_2/T_1=p_2/p_1$. With $T_1=293.15$ K: $T_2=293.15\times2=586.3$ K $=313\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. **A** wrongly doubles $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ directly (40 $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$). **B** reports the kelvin value as if it were $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. **D** halves the result. The trap is forgetting to convert to kelvin.
practice.theory: At constant volume for an ideal gas, $p/T=$ const, so $T_2=T_1(p_2/p_1)$ with $T$ in kelvin.

practice.prompt: For an ideal gas, the equation $du=c_v\,dT$ can be used:

options.content: only when kinetic-energy changes are negligible
only during a constant-pressure process if PE changes are negligible

practice.explanation: Since $u=u(T)$ for an ideal gas, $du=c_v\,dT$ holds on **any** path — it is a property relation, like the equation of state. **B** confuses the subscript $v$ with a process restriction. **C** and **D** bolt on irrelevant KE/PE conditions; the relation concerns internal energy and temperature only.
practice.theory: Ideal gas: $u=u(T)$, $h=h(T)$ $\Rightarrow$ $\Delta u=c_v\Delta T$ and $\Delta h=c_p\Delta T$ for every process.

practice.prompt: For an ideal gas with $R=287\ \mathrm{J/(kg\cdot K)}$ and $k=1.4$, the value of $c_v$ is closest to:
practice.explanation: $c_v=R/(k-1)=287/0.4=717.5\ \mathrm{J/(kg\cdot K)}$. **A** is $c_p=kR/(k-1)=1004.5$, the constant-pressure heat. **B** uses $R(k-1)=287\times0.4$ by mistake. **D** halves $c_p$. Check: $c_p-c_v=1004.5-717.5=287=R$. $\checkmark$

practice.prompt: A tank holds 1 kg of water as a saturated liquid–vapor mixture at 1 bar ($h_f=417$, $h_g=2675$ kJ/kg), quality $x=0.75$. The heat needed at constant pressure to reach saturated vapor is:
practice.explanation: $Q=m(h_g-h_A)=m(1-x)h_{fg}=1\times0.25\times(2675-417)=564.5$ kJ. **A** uses $x=0.75$ instead of $(1-x)=0.25$ ($0.75\times2258=1693.5$). **C** is $h_A$ itself, not the change. **D** is the full latent heat $h_{fg}$ as if starting from $x=0$.
practice.theory: At constant pressure $Q=m\,\Delta h$. For wet steam $h_A=h_f+x\,h_{fg}$, so to reach $g$: $Q=m(1-x)h_{fg}$.

practice.prompt: Water at 10 bar: $v_f=0.00113$, $v_g=0.1944\ \mathrm{m^3/kg}$. A closed rigid vessel of $0.5\ \mathrm{m^3}$ contains 5 kg of this water at 10 bar. The quality is closest to:
practice.explanation: $v=V/m=0.5/5=0.10\ \mathrm{m^3/kg}$, which lies between $v_f$ and $v_g$ (two-phase). $x=(v-v_f)/(v_g-v_f)=(0.10-0.00113)/(0.1944-0.00113)=0.09887/0.19327\approx0.51$. **B** mistakes $v=0.10$ for $x$. **C** uses $v/v_g$. **D** uses $v_f$ in place of the difference. Always compute $v$ first, then locate it in the dome.
practice.source: Lecture Thermodynamics 1 (quality, tables); Polito tutorial style

practice.prompt: Which list contains only **path functions** (not properties)?
practice.explanation: Work, heat, and entropy production $\sigma$ all depend on the path taken, not just the endpoints — they are path functions with inexact differentials. **A** lists three state properties. **C** mixes in $U$, a property. **D** includes $T$, a property. State functions: $U,H,S,T,p,v$.
practice.theory: Path functions ($Q, W, \sigma$) depend on the process; state/point functions ($U,H,S,p,T,v$) depend only on the state.

practice.prompt: Inside the vapor dome, during boiling at constant pressure, what happens to the temperature?

options.content: It increases steadily as heat is added.

practice.explanation: In the two-phase region a constant-pressure process is also constant-temperature: $T$ is pinned at $T_{sat}(p)$ while added heat converts liquid to vapor (latent heat) and $v$ increases. **A** describes single-phase heating. **C** and **D** contradict the saturation behavior of a pure substance.
practice.theory: Two-phase: $p$ and $T$ are coupled; at fixed $p$ boiling occurs at fixed $T_{sat}$, only $v$ (and $x$) change.
practice.source: Lecture Thermodynamics 1 (phase change at constant pressure)

practice.prompt: Air ($R=287\ \mathrm{J/(kg\cdot K)}$) fills a $0.20\ \mathrm{m^3}$ rigid tank at 8 bar and 27 $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. The mass of air is closest to:
practice.explanation: $m=\dfrac{pV}{RT}=\dfrac{800000\times0.20}{287\times300.15}=\dfrac{160000}{86143}=1.86$ kg. **A** halves the result by a slip. **C** uses $T=27$ K (forgetting to convert to kelvin entirely), wildly inflating $m$. **D** uses a wrong $R$. The trap is again kelvin: $T=27+273.15=300.15$ K.

practice.prompt: At 5 bar, water tables give $T_{sat}=151.8$ $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. A state at 5 bar and 200 $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is:

options.content: a saturated mixture with $x$ between 0 and 1

practice.explanation: At a given pressure, $T>T_{sat}$ means the substance is past complete vaporization: superheated vapor. **A** would require $T<T_{sat}$. **B** requires $T=T_{sat}$ exactly (two-phase sits on the saturation line). **D** is wrong — the critical point of water is 374 $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, 220 bar. Use the superheated-vapor table for this state.
practice.theory: At fixed $p$: $T<T_{sat}$ $\Rightarrow$ subcooled liquid; $T=T_{sat}$ $\Rightarrow$ two-phase; $T>T_{sat}$ $\Rightarrow$ superheated vapor.

practice.prompt: Steam-table rows at 0.10 MPa give $h_g=2675.5$ kJ/kg and at 0.20 MPa give $h_g=2706.7$ kJ/kg. By linear interpolation, $h_g$ at 0.15 MPa is closest to:
practice.explanation: Linear interpolation: $h_g=2675.5+(2706.7-2675.5)\times\dfrac{0.15-0.10}{0.20-0.10}=2675.5+31.2\times0.5=2691.1$ kJ/kg. **A** and **B** just copy an endpoint. **D** extrapolates beyond the upper row. Halfway in $p$ gives halfway in $h_g$.
practice.theory: Linear interpolation: $y=y_1+(y_2-y_1)\frac{x-x_1}{x_2-x_1}$ between bracketing table rows.

practice.prompt: The defining equation of enthalpy is:
practice.explanation: By definition $H=U+pV$, or per unit mass $h=u+pv$. **A** has the wrong sign. **C** is dimensionally and conceptually wrong. **D** is the internal energy of an ideal gas (referenced from 0), not the definition of enthalpy. For an ideal gas this further gives $h=u+RT$.
practice.theory: Enthalpy $h\equiv u+pv$; for an ideal gas $pv=RT$ so $h=u(T)+RT=h(T)$.

practice.prompt: For an ideal gas $k=c_p/c_v$ is always:
practice.explanation: Since $c_p=c_v+R$ and $R>0$, $c_p>c_v$, so $k=c_p/c_v>1$ always (e.g. air $k=1.4$, monatomic gases $k\approx1.67$). **A** and **B** contradict $c_p>c_v$. **D** confuses the dimensionless ratio with the gas constant $R$.

exam.title: Locating a state and finding quality (water)
exam.statement: A rigid closed vessel of volume $V=0.20\ \mathrm{m^3}$ contains $m=2.0$ kg of water at $p=10$ bar. From the saturation table at 10 bar: $v_f=1.127\times10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^3/kg}$, $v_g=0.1944\ \mathrm{m^3/kg}$, $h_f=762.6$ kJ/kg, $h_g=2776.2$ kJ/kg, $T_{sat}=179.9$ $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. (a) Determine the region and quality $x$. (b) Find the specific enthalpy $h$ of the mixture. (c) State the temperature.

steps.title: Identify the region
steps.content: Compare with saturation: $v_f=0.001127\le v=0.10\le v_g=0.1944$, so the state lies **inside the dome** — a two-phase saturated mixture.

steps.title: Temperature
steps.content: Inside the dome $T=T_{sat}(10\,\mathrm{bar})=179.9$ $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ — fixed by the pressure alone.

exam.tips: First thing every time: get $v=V/m$ and compare to $v_f, v_g$. Remember Polito tables list $v\times10^3$. Inside the dome the temperature is fixed by pressure alone, so you do not interpolate for it.

exam.title: Ideal-gas state and caloric properties
exam.statement: A closed rigid tank of $V=0.5\ \mathrm{m^3}$ contains air ($R=287\ \mathrm{J/(kg\cdot K)}$, $c_v=717.5$, $c_p=1004.5\ \mathrm{J/(kg\cdot K)}$) at $p_1=3$ bar, $t_1=27$ $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. It is heated until $p_2=6$ bar. Find (a) the mass of air, (b) the final temperature $T_2$, (c) the change in internal energy $\Delta U$ and (d) the change in enthalpy $\Delta H$.
exam.finalAnswer: $m\approx1.74$ kg; $T_2\approx600$ K (327 $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$); $\Delta U\approx375$ kJ; $\Delta H\approx525$ kJ.
exam.tips: $\Delta u=c_v\Delta T$ and $\Delta h=c_p\Delta T$ are valid even though this is a constant-volume process — they depend only on $T$ for an ideal gas. Convert to kelvin before using the gas law; a $\Delta T$ in K equals the $\Delta T$ in $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.

exam.title: Heat to vaporize and then superheat steam
exam.statement: In a closed piston–cylinder at constant pressure $p=2$ bar, 0.5 kg of water starts as a wet mixture with quality $x_1=0.40$ and is heated until it becomes saturated vapor ($x_2=1$). At 2 bar: $h_f=504.7$, $h_g=2706.7$ kJ/kg, $T_{sat}=120.2$ $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$; superheated steam at 2 bar, 300 $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ has $h=3071.8$ kJ/kg. (a) Find $h_1$. (b) Find the heat added to reach saturated vapor. (c) How much extra heat to continue to 300 $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$?

steps.title: Heat to saturated vapor (constant p ⟹ Q = ΔH)
Extra heat to superheat to 300 °C
steps.content: Beyond $x=1$ you leave the dome and read the superheated table: $Q_{extra}=m(h_{300}-h_g)=0.5(3071.8-2706.7)=0.5\times365.1=182.6$ kJ.

steps.title: Temperature path check
steps.content: From $x_1$ to $x_2$ the temperature stays at $T_{sat}=120.2$ $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (latent heat). Only after $x=1$ does $T$ climb toward 300 $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (sensible superheat).

exam.finalAnswer: $h_1=1385.5$ kJ/kg; heat to saturated vapor $Q=660.6$ kJ; extra heat to 300 $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ $\approx182.6$ kJ (total $\approx843$ kJ from start), switching from the saturation table to the superheated-vapor table.
exam.tips: Constant-pressure heat equals the enthalpy change, $Q=m\,\Delta h$. While inside the dome $T$ is fixed; heat goes into latent vaporization. The instant $x$ reaches 1 you must switch tables — the most common error is trying to push quality past 1.

exam.title: Describe the p-v-T surface and the vapor dome
exam.statement: On a $p$–$v$ diagram, sketch the vapor dome for water and a constant-pressure heating line that starts in the subcooled-liquid region and ends in superheated vapor. Label the saturated-liquid line, saturated-vapor line, critical point, and the three regions. Explain (i) why $p$ and $T$ are not independent inside the dome, (ii) the role of quality $x$, and (iii) which property model (ideal gas vs tables) you would use on each side of the dome.
exam.given: Qualitative; use definitions of saturation, quality, and the state postulate.

steps.title: Draw the dome
steps.content: A bell-shaped curve: left branch is the **saturated-liquid line** ($x=0$, subscript $f$), right branch is the **saturated-vapor line** ($x=1$, subscript $g$). They meet at the **critical point** (top, $T_c\approx374$ $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, $p_c\approx220$ bar for water). Left of the dome = subcooled liquid; under the dome = two-phase mixture; right = superheated vapor.

steps.title: Draw the constant-p line
steps.content: A horizontal line in $p$–$v$ (since $p$=const). Moving right at fixed $p$: in the liquid region $v$ barely grows while $T$ rises to $T_{sat}$; crossing the $f$ line into the dome the line stays at the same $p$ and same $T=T_{sat}$ while $v$ grows from $v_f$ to $v_g$ (boiling); past the $g$ line, $v$ and $T$ rise again (superheating).

steps.title: (i) Why p and T are coupled inside the dome
steps.content: During a phase change at fixed pressure the temperature is locked at $T_{sat}(p)$. So specifying $p$ already fixes $T$ — they are not independent. By Gibbs' rule $V=C-F+2=1-2+2=1$: only one intensive property is free.

steps.title: (ii) Role of quality
steps.content: Because $(p,T)$ cannot locate a two-phase state, we add **quality** $x=m_{vap}/m$. Then any property follows $\varphi=(1-x)\varphi_f+x\,\varphi_g$ for $\varphi\in\{v,u,h,s\}$. $x$ tells you how far across the dome (from $f$ to $g$) the state sits.

steps.title: (iii) Which model where
steps.content: Far to the right and at low pressure / high temperature, the vapor behaves nearly ideally: $pv=RT$ with $\Delta u=c_v\Delta T$, $\Delta h=c_p\Delta T$ is acceptable. Near or inside the dome (and for the liquid) you must use **property tables** — the ideal-gas law is invalid there.

exam.finalAnswer: A correct sketch shows the dome with $f$/$g$ lines and critical point, plus a horizontal constant-$p$ line crossing all three regions with a flat $T$ segment inside the dome. Key statements: inside the dome $p$ and $T$ are dependent (Gibbs $V=1$); quality $x$ supplies the missing intensive coordinate via $\varphi=(1-x)\varphi_f+x\varphi_g$; use tables near the dome but the ideal-gas model is acceptable for vapor far from it.
exam.tips: Examiners look for: correct dome shape, the FLAT temperature plateau during boiling, correct region labels, and the link between Gibbs' rule and the need for quality. Stating that a constant-$p$ line in the dome is also constant-$T$ earns easy marks.
